rule_text: Business capability modules are the primary architecture boundary.
A module MUST own its application services, domain rules, data contracts, API contracts, and tests for its capability.
A module MUST NOT directly mutate another module's owned data.
Cross-module behavior MUST use explicit application contracts, domain events, or documented integration points.
Shared modules MUST provide infrastructure behavior and MUST NOT own product behavior decisions.
Platform-scope modules MUST NOT own tenant operational data.
Tenant-scope modules MUST NOT own platform control-plane data.
Each business module SHOULD be structured around domain, application, infrastructure, and presentation/API concerns.
Domain rules MUST NOT depend on database, HTTP, UI, or framework-specific implementation details.
Application services coordinate use cases and enforce module-level workflow rules.
Infrastructure code implements persistence, external integrations, messaging, and framework adapters.
API endpoints MUST call application services rather than directly mutating persistence models.
Module tests MUST cover domain rules, application use cases, API contracts, and persistence boundaries when touched.
Domain layers MUST NOT depend on Infrastructure or Api layers.
Application layers MUST NOT depend on Api layers.
Api layers MUST NOT contain domain behavior.
Infrastructure layers MAY depend on their own module Application and Domain layers.
FastAPI entrypoints MAY reference module Api and Infrastructure routers.
Cross-module direct Infrastructure layer dependency is forbidden.
Shared core packages MUST NOT contain IoTable product decisions.
Platform database connection configuration MUST be resolved by app.main and app.worker configurations.
Tenant database connection resolution MUST use tenant context and tenant database registry.
Secrets MUST NOT be stored in source code or specification examples.
Platform and tenant frontend applications MUST NOT be hosted inside backend repositories.
Redis configuration MUST be resolved by backend configuration and MUST be treated as ephemeral infrastructure.
Celery configuration MUST be resolved by app.worker configuration.
platform_web and tenant_web MUST be separate Next.js applications.
platform_web MUST be served only from platform.iotables.net.
tenant_web MUST be served only from tenant domains matching {tenant}.iotables.net.
platform_web MUST call only platform API routes under /api/platform/**.
tenant_web MUST NOT call platform API routes.
platform_web MUST NOT include tenant menu, order, station, checkout, or customer session screens.
tenant_web MUST NOT include tenant lifecycle, provisioning, schema job, platform settings, or platform audit screens.
Platform authentication state MUST be separate from tenant authentication state.
Platform cookies, tenant staff cookies, and customer guest continuity state MUST use separate names and scopes.
Shared UI code MAY exist only as framework-independent packages without product authorization behavior.
IoTable backend implementation MUST use Python 3.14 and FastAPI.
IoTable frontend implementation MUST use Next.js 16.2.4 and React 19.2.5.
IoTable frontend implementation MUST use TypeScript 6.0.3.
IoTable frontend styling baseline MUST use Tailwind CSS v4.0.
IoTable Node tooling MUST use Node.js 24 LTS and pnpm 11.0.3.
IoTable persistent relational storage MUST use PostgreSQL 17.
IoTable frontend deployables MUST remain Next.js applications named platform_web and tenant_web.
A version change in this baseline MUST update every dependent architecture, implementation, testing, and deployment specification in the same specification change.
Multi-tenant SSL certificates MUST be provisioned dynamically and automatically using Caddy On-Demand TLS.
The reverse proxy MUST validate the existence of the tenant slug via the platform API before completing the TLS handshake.
Subdomain routing MUST map {tenant}.iotables.net/app/** and /console/** to the tenant_web Next.js application.
Platform routing MUST map platform.iotables.net/** to the platform_web Next.js application.
Frontend UI MUST dynamically resolve user theme preferences (light/dark mode) and store it locally.
Brand colors and background variables MUST strictly use HSL/Hex values defined in ui_design_system_tokens.
Interactive UI elements MUST use micro-animations with transition-duration of 200ms and ease-in-out timing function.
A module MUST own its primary data contracts.
A module MUST NOT write another module's owned data except through that module's defined API or application service contract.
Platform-scope modules MUST NOT directly own tenant operational data.
Tenant-scope modules MUST NOT directly own platform control-plane data.
Shared modules MUST provide infrastructure behavior without owning business decisions.
Excluded modules MUST remain out of implementation scope unless a new owner decision changes the product baseline.
Every YAML specification file MUST declare its matching JSON Schema path.
Every specification file MUST be written as current final system state.
Every added field MUST be represented in the matching schema.
Every open product behavior MUST be recorded as an open decision.
Every implementation task MUST include done_when criteria before it can be used by a coding agent.
The coding agent MUST implement tasks in dependency order.
The coding agent MUST NOT implement unspecified API behavior.
The coding agent MUST NOT add packages that are not allowed by the specification.
The coding agent MUST run verification commands when specified.
The coding agent MUST report a blocker instead of guessing.
A source is evidence, not inspiration.
Product behavior MUST come from a project owner decision or an owner-accepted product source.
Technical facts that are version-sensitive MUST be checked against current authoritative sources before entering the specification.
Reference products MAY influence comparison, but MUST NOT be copied as requirements.
A source conflict MUST be recorded as a decision item before the specification is frozen.
No requirement may depend on external context that is not represented in the repository.
Every implementation task MUST identify the verification tiers affected by the task.
A task that changes a public or internal API contract MUST include API contract verification.
A task that changes database schema, indexes, constraints, seed data, or ownership rules MUST include database contract verification.
A task that changes authentication, authorization, customer access, device identity, token handling, or secret handling MUST include security verification.
A task that changes a user-facing workflow MUST include workflow-level verification.
A task that changes deployment, health checks, configuration, background jobs, or recovery behavior MUST include operational verification.
Specification validation MUST pass before implementation verification is considered complete.
IoTable backend MUST expose /health, /health/live, and /health/ready.
/health MUST behave as a liveness-compatible alias.
/health/live MUST NOT fail because of database, cache, background job, or external dependency unavailability.
/health/ready MUST fail when a required dependency prevents safe request handling.
Liveness MUST check process responsiveness, not business readiness.
A temporary database outage MUST NOT cause /health/live to fail.
A saturated background job queue MUST NOT cause /health/live to fail.
Readiness MUST verify dependencies required for safe request handling.
Readiness MUST fail when the required database for the addressed scope is unreachable.
Readiness MUST fail when required migrations are not applied.
Readiness MUST fail when required runtime configuration is missing.
Readiness MUST NOT include business-state checks such as open order count, active table count, menu item count, or tenant revenue.
A readiness request for platform.iotables.net MUST evaluate platform-scope readiness.
A readiness request for a tenant host matching {tenant}.iotables.net MUST evaluate readiness for that resolved tenant.
Release verification MUST check readiness for platform.iotables.net and at least one provisioned active tenant host.
Health responses MUST use application/health+json.
Health responses MUST include status, service, version, releaseId, and checks.
A failed health response MUST use HTTP 503.
A health response MUST NOT include secrets, connection strings, token values, customer session identifiers, bill content, order content, or firmware secrets.
/health and /health/live MAY be exposed through the reverse proxy for uptime monitoring.
/health/ready MUST be restricted to trusted operational callers because readiness detail can expose internal dependency names.
Health endpoints MUST NOT require platform_owner or tenant staff authentication when called by trusted operational infrastructure.
Release verification MUST include liveness and readiness checks against the deployed backend.
Release verification MUST prove that readiness can fail closed for a required dependency.
Backups MUST be retained for 30 days.
IoTable recovery point objective MUST be 24 hours.
IoTable recovery time objective MUST be 4 hours.
A recovery drill MUST be performed at least once every 90 days.
Release verification MUST confirm that health-check release gate checks defined in specs/05_testing_operations.yaml pass.
A release MUST NOT knowingly ship with a broken backup path.
A release MUST record an operational blocker when current measurements show a breach of a defined availability, latency, device, or recovery target.
The platform control plane MUST NOT expose tenant operational workflows.
The platform control plane MUST NOT provide menu, order, station, checkout, or customer session management for a tenant.
The platform control plane MAY inspect tenant lifecycle, provisioning, schema, activation, audit, and health metadata.
The platform control plane MUST be served only from platform.iotables.net.
Platform routes MUST use platform API routes under /api/platform/**.

The platform control plane MUST use a Workspace-centric SaaS layout consisting of a three-pane view (Left Navigation Sidebar, Middle Master Grid/List, and Right Contextual Inspector Panel) to maximize context preservation and prevent full-page reloads.

The Right Contextual Inspector Panel MUST display all selected tenant-specific details (Overview, Lifecycle, Provisioning, Database, Owner Activation, Jobs, Audit, and Health) using in-panel horizontal tab navigation to ensure a unified master-detail interface.

Form submissions and interactive creation processes (such as Tenant Creation) MUST be built as overlay modal dialogs or multi-step onboarding wizards instead of separate full-page routes to retain parent view context.
The platform control plane navigation and right-drawer states MUST be URL-aware (via query parameters or route interception) so that deep-linking and browser history support work seamlessly.
A global keyboard-accessible Command Palette (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) MUST be integrated to allow platform owners to instantly search and switch between tenants, jobs, or settings pages.
Tenant display name is required.
Tenant owner full name is required.
Tenant owner email is required and MUST be a valid email address.
Tenant slug MUST contain only lowercase ascii letters, digits, and hyphens.
Tenant slug MUST be globally unique.
Tenant slug MUST NOT be editable after tenant creation is confirmed.
Tenant domain MUST be derived from tenant slug.
Tenant database name MUST be derived from tenant slug.
Platform owner MUST NOT manually enter tenant database name.
Platform owner MUST NOT manually enter tenant id, provisioning job id, activation token, or connection metadata.
Default locale, currency, timezone, and initial plan MUST have explicit values at creation time.
Tenant slug MUST be globally unique and immutable after creation.
Tenant domain MUST be globally unique and follow {tenant}.iotables.net.
Tenant database name MUST be globally unique.
Platform audit log MUST be append-only.
platform_idempotency_keys MUST prevent duplicate execution of mutation requests with the same idempotency key.
connection_secret_ref MUST reference external secret storage and MUST NOT contain plaintext credentials.
Tenant lifecycle active MUST NOT imply tenant operations active.
Tenant owner activation invite records MUST be stored in the tenant database, not the platform database.
ESP32 devices MUST NOT generate QR tokens.
ESP32 devices MUST display only server-generated QR payloads.
Device secret MUST be revocable.
A device MUST be assigned to exactly one tenant table.
A table MAY have at most one active QR display device.
Device reconnect MUST NOT rotate QR by itself.
Server MUST remain source of truth for QR state.
Device messages MUST be tenant-scoped and table-scoped.
Revoked devices MUST NOT receive QR payloads.
Firmware artifacts, Wi-Fi credentials, and device secrets MUST NOT be logged in plaintext.
Firmware artifacts MUST be treated as tenant secrets.
All WebSocket communication between ESP32 and the backend MUST use the unified JSON event frame format containing exact 'event' and 'payload' keys.
ESP32 devices MUST send a 'heartbeat' event frame every 60 seconds.
The backend MUST disconnect the WebSocket if no frame is received within 120 seconds.
The backend MUST send 'qr_update' frames when a customer session begins or rotates, containing a valid target URL and epoch expiration timestamp.
ESP32 firmware MUST implement an exponential backoff reconnection algorithm with random jitter up to a maximum of 300 seconds when disconnected.
The WebSocket connection manager MUST NOT queue historical 'qr_update' frames for offline or disconnected table devices.

For each active device, the server MUST buffer only the single, latest active 'qr_update' frame payload in Redis (with TTL matching the QR code expiration). All older or expired update payloads for that device MUST be overwritten or discarded immediately to prevent event storming and hardware memory exhaustion upon reconnection.
Platform and tenant databases MUST target PostgreSQL 17 exclusively.
Schema migrations MUST be sequential, using Alembic with strict file naming V{Version}__{Description}.sql.
All migrations MUST execute within an explicit database transaction unless they include commands like CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY which PostgreSQL prohibits inside transactions.
Schema upgrades MUST strictly follow the Expand-Contract pattern to ensure database compatibility with both currently running and next-deploying API code versions.
Naming of indexes, foreign keys, and unique constraints MUST follow deterministic naming schemas: 'idx_{table}_{column}', 'fk_{table}_{referenced_table}', and 'uq_{table}_{column}'.

A failed schema migration on a specific tenant database MUST pause orchestration for that tenant immediately but MUST NOT block pending schema upgrades for other, independent tenant databases (failure isolation).

To recover from transient network drops, the schema migration task engine MAY perform at most 1 automatic retry; if it fails again, it MUST flag the tenant registry with database_status = 'migration_failed' and schema_drift = true.

Re-triggering a failed schema migration from the platform control plane MUST resume exactly from the failed migration version, leveraging transactional state tracking, and MUST NOT attempt to re-apply already completed migration steps.
